text: Velkommen til D&B og spesial episode av podcastet Utbyttet, hvor vi diskusser utløk for LNG shipping.
Jeg er Marius Brunhaugen fra D&B Markets, og today I'm joined by Paul Wogen, the CEO of Gaslog.
Hvordan er du, Paul?
Yes, good morning, very well, thank you. Nice to be here.
It's great to have you with us. We are also joined by my colleague and shipping analyst here at the D&B Markets, Matz B.
All well at the office, Matz?
Yes, certainly, sun is shining over Oslo today.
That's good to hear. So the way we'll do this is that Matz and I will take turns asking questions to you today, Paul.
I'll kick off straight away and I thank you for the listeners that are not too familiar with the space.
Could you start by explaining in brief what is LNG and what is Gaslog's role in the industry?
Yes, of course. Well, LNG is quite simply natural gas, which is super cooled to minus 162 degrees centigrade to turn into liquid
and put on vessels and shipped around the world.
Basically, it's a way to use gas that would otherwise be surplus to needs or stranded.
If there's no demand centre close by, that's close enough to be supplied by pipeline,
then it's liquefied, put on our ships and then we take it wherever the buyer wants it,
and it's then discharged ashore where it's regassified and put into the grid and used as any other natural gas.
So, basically, Gaslog's role in the industry is a floating pipeline.
Paul, the LNG space has traditionally been one with very long contracts for freight,
maybe reaching as much as 20 years or longer.
Recent years has seen a shift with more volumes being moved as spotcargos
and contract durations are getting shorter as a trend.
Hvordan ser du denne developpenne i forhold?
Hvordan har du i Gaslog tatt til å posisjere denne space?
Ja, det er en bra forskning. The market has certainly changed over the past 20 years.
Hvis du går tilbake til LNG først startet, er det veldig point-to-point.
Hvis du har et nytt fasilitet i Southeast Asia,
så er det å bygge en japanisk fasilitet, for eksempel,
og tar alle kontrakter til volumet for de kommende 20 år,
og så går de tilbake og tilbake,
og så er det å begynne å begynne å begynne å begynne å begynne å begynne å begynne å begynne.
Over de seneste flere år har det definitivt vært andre.
LNG har blitt mye mer globalt kommoditet, mye mer tradert.
Så har det vært en åpning av bare single-point-movement av kargos.
Men stort, i hverandre av kommoditeten er det underpindt
med langsdags- og skjølgjørsagd.
Så i dag, rundt 70-80% av alle LNG skjølget,
er det stort under langsdags- og skjølgjørs kontrakter.
Det er kanskje ikke 20 år, kanskje 10 eller 12 eller 15 år.
Og simpelthen, rundt 70-80% av globalt skjølgjørsagd,
og en mirro i kommoditeten,
er det også å operere på en form av multi-year charter.
Spottmarketing og short-termmarketing, og i dette market,
som andre i skjølget, når vi prøver om det,
vi prøver å prøve om noe under 5 år,
er gammel.
Trader er gammel, portfølger og skjølger er gammel.
De vil...
De skjølger, hvis du liker, optionalt til marketing,
og de skjølger produktet.
Men om du vil bygge en multi-millionfacilitet,
til å likere gaset,
du må bruke noen typ av lang term kontrakter
under det, for å finansere det.
Så i generelt,
jeg tror ikke at det skal være en moviment
fullt av lang term kontrakter.
Noget av de skjølger, ikke?
Noget av de grønne oljefaktene,
Tari, for eksempel, kan forstå å underleve
de egne facilitetene.
Men i generelt,
de som vil bygge de LNG facilitetene,
de vil selvfølgelig bruke noen
lang term facilitet,
for å underleve de facilitetene,
og jeg tror de vil lide
noen av de produktet for å være børn,
soldet i året.
Så definitivt en moviment
til kjølig term kontrakter
og mer fleksibilitet.
Så Paul, gas har vært lyftet
som kanskje en fosilfølg
som har en roll til å spille
denne forfølgelige.
Kan du skjølge ut denne utdelingen
for gas-demand-grønne
for å se de næste decadeen?
Ja, jeg tror, som du sier,
det er en av de flere
hydrocarboner som er virkelig
for å grønne.
Det er en av de fastestgrønne hydrocarboner.
Wod Mac, for eksempel,
Wod Mackenzie, forkastes
LNG-demandgrønne omkring
4-5 % per annum
for de næste 10 år.
Og denne demanden
er primært dribbende
med et par ting,
med å svåre ekonomiske
i Asien,
begynne å skjølge
fra kold til gas
i energi
for elektriske generasjoner.
Det er også
om
kold til gas
i Europa.
Vi har sett mye av det
over de seneste årene,
kold til gas
som LNG-demandgrønne
har vært bedre.
Og det er også
massivt koldt i China.
Nå kjølger det
med gas som en måte
å bringe ned denne koldt.
Og jeg tror at det er noe for gas
som er så god,
er at det er en idealt fuel
for koldt.
Det er en god måte
å begynne energi
når denne koldt ikke blåer
eller denne koldt ikke begynner.
Gasfiede koldt generasjoner
er veldig kveld å bringe online
når det er begynnet.
Jeg tror at denne koldt
å arbeide i tandem med koldt
er en av de ting
som er også helpet
for koldt-demandgrønne.
Og det er signifikant koldt
for koldt-demandgrønne.
Jeg tror også at det er energi
og denne koldt-demandgrønne.
Det er denne koldt-demandgrønne
der vi tror at denne koldt
å begynne koldt
er i denne koldt-demandgrønne.
Selv om denne koldt-demandgrønne
for koldt-demandgrønne.
Men du ser at denne koldt
er utgjort i
koldt-demandgrønne.
Så de sort av
faktorer som
driver koldt-demandgrønne.
Så vi er ganske
å begynne koldt-demandgrønne
for koldt-demandgrønne
for koldt-demandgrønne.
Ja, Paul.
Denne koldt-demandgrønne
for koldt-demandgrønne
og kanskje mer i de seneste året.
Hvis du ser på denne koldt-demandgrønne
der i historiske koldt-demandgrønne
på bare 2 dollar per meter
eller
rundt 7 % av Brent
som er nødt til å ha
historisk koldt-demandgrønne
rundt 13-14 %.
Nå er det for oss
at energimarket
i generelt er oversupplytet
men kanskje mer så
på denne koldt-demandgrønne.
Hvis du ser på denne koldt-demandgrønne
for koldt-demandgrønne
hvordan ser du
denne koldt-demandgrønne
med koldt-demandgrønne
og at
koldt-demandgrønne
og koldt-demandgrønne
er negativ for koldt-demandgrønne
som du normalt ser
i koldt-demandgrønne.
Ja, ja.
Jeg tror
det som er interessant
er for koldt-demandgrønne
det ikke er en
koldt-demandgrønne
som gjør denne koldt-demandgrønne.
Vi har sett
koldt-demandgrønne
hvis de
var koldt-demandgrønne
å fortsatte med koldt-demandgrønne
i April.
Indier i koldt 1.
Befør de var koldt-demandgrønne
var koldt-demandgrønne
37% koldt-demandgrønne
og jeg tror det er
en fantastisk eksempel
for koldt-demandgrønne
som historisk fremgjør
koldt-demandgrønne
compared to what we're saying, the absolute demand destruction for oil, for example.
For LNG, it's holding up quite well.
The problem is the supply that has come on stream last year, and it's coming on this stream,
is coming on stream this year, is very high.
And as you say, that supply against a sort of flat to slightly increasing demand
is what's pushing the pricing down.
The best response or the best way to counter low prices is low prices,
because eventually it creates the demand.
But I don't think the pricing we're seeing at the moment is sustainable.
But we do need LNG to be, I think, relatively well priced
to continue to be able to take market share from coal, et cetera.
So you really need to get to, I think, to a balance where you're seeing
LNG pricing somewhere between sort of seven to ten dollars longer term.
That I think allows the producers to be profitable
and I think makes it competitive against some of the other sources.
From our point of view, as a ship on is yes,
the prices we're seeing at the moment, especially when you're seeing JKM
and TTF prices dropping even below the sort of Henry Hub pricing,
makes it very difficult in terms of shipping, in terms of being able to make a margin.
What has been interesting this year, though, is that if you look at the amount of short term
cargoes in the first quarter, it was up quite substantially on the first quarter of 2019.
So there has continued to be a demand for shipping, albeit at lower rates.
From Gaslog's point of view, it doesn't affect us as much as some other companies
in that the vast majority of our ships are on long term contracts at fixed rates,
which has nothing to do with the commodity pricing or anything like that.
So whilst we have some ships trading in the spot market
and we would love those vessels to be earning more,
the majority of our business is coming from that longer term contracted business.
I think the final thing on the demand supply side is what we're seeing right now
is I think the pandemic is likely to push out new project sanctions.
So I was going into this year expected to be talk of 50, 70 million tonnes of new project sanctions this year.
I think most of those get pushed out and we don't see those coming in now until mid-20s,
which I think gives breathing space for the market to rebalance in the short to medium term.
Paul, would you say there is so much oversupply
that there is a risk of curtailed LNG production volumes in 2020,
much like the oil markets is experiencing at the moment?
Yes, I mean we're starting to see reports of cargo cancellations.
Primarily I think from the US for the summer,
there have been talk of potentially 20 cargoes not being lifted in June and July.
Not clear if all those cancellations will actually happen
because the way it works is that certainly for quite a few of the projects in the US,
the, take Chania for example, Chania has a tolling agreement whereby it gets paid
for the production whether or not the customer takes it.
If the customer decides not to take it, they've already been paid
so they can then also decide well now I've been paid to process this,
do I want to process the cargo and try and make a turn on it myself.
For example, you hear maybe 20 cargoes have been postponed in June,
some of those may get taken, but they may not as well.
To put it into perspective as well, we did some sort of work on it.
We think if you saw 20 to 25 cargoes a month for the next two, three months,
not be moved out of the US, it's equivalent we believe to around 10 vessels
out of a fleet of around 500 ships.
Not a huge amount, but still I don't think we would prefer all that cargo to be moved.
It certainly helps in terms of tightening the market, but from a shipping side,
it's not as drastic if those figures maintain as perhaps first years.
Jumping from the demand side of ships to supply side,
there have been very limited new gold orders in 2020,
which can be seen as a positive after you had 66 large LNGCs ordered in 2018
and 50 last year in 2019.
That adds about or even more than 25% of the fleet in terms of carrying capacity.
If you look at the order book now, one third is not committed to long term sharkers,
but what should we expect from ordering activity going forward?
Will it bounce back, do you believe, when economic activity picks up again?
Well, I suppose the quick answer to that is I hope not,
but we've been really encouraged by the recent slowdown in the orders
and I'm not being a Monday morning quarter back here.
I think if you look back over our early releases over the last 4, 5, 6 quarters,
we've been saying for a long time, we think that we're in a good position
from a new building situation, we shouldn't be ordering new ships.
Certainly we ourselves haven't ordered anything over the last 18, 24 months.
We've got 5 new orders still to come,
but all of them are on long term charters over 70 years, nothing open to the market.
We've been trying to say for a while that we need to have a restraint in new ordering
because what tends to happen is that the vessels will deliver on time
and the projects will drop back.
There has never been a point where looking forward,
there have been too many ships for the amount of product coming,
but there's always been a mismatch in timing
where the ships come too early and therefore are adding to the market.
I think looking forward, as you said quite rightly there,
up to a third of the ships are not fixed on long term charters
or open to the market and so even as we start to see activity picking up
and people looking to reinstate projects which then maybe they postponed,
my hope is that we won't see a rush to the shipyards,
35 or so open new bills that we see on the order book right now
will be fixed against those new requirements
without having to go and do new buildings.
So that's what we hope, be interesting to see,
but certainly if you look out over the next four to five years,
we'll need every ship on the water.
What we need to make sure is that we try to get those ships to deliver
as and when required and not get ahead of the demand
which of course then creates the pressure on the shipping rates.
In other shipping segments a boat is a boat you can say
and it earns at least about the same no matter when it was built.
As Matt says explained to me, LNG is very different
with rapid changes to vessel technology in the last 20 years.
Can you explain the dynamics of the market
and also what sort of vessels you have in the gas log fleet?
Ja, sure.
There are basically three principle designs in the market right now
and starting from the earlier ones through to today.
So the earlier ones were the steam vessels
which were up to about 2005, 2006.
Then we saw what we call trifuel diesel electric engines coming in
and then we have seen sort of two stroke slow speed engines.
So what's the primary difference there?
Well, it's really around fuel consumption.
The steam turbines consume approximately 180 tonnes of fuel
oil equivalent to a day.
The TFDEs are around 120, 130 tonnes, so 50 to 60 tonnes less.
And the two strokes are around 100 tonnes.
So 20 to 30 tonnes less than the TFDEs
and 80 tonnes less than the steam turbines.
So you can see the increase in efficiency
nearly harving of fuel consumption over the last 15 years.
The second thing is that the containment systems.
So basically the ships are huge thermos flasks.
You put the liquid in there.
You don't have it under pressure.
You don't refrigerate on the ship.
You just try to keep it as cool as possible.
So you have a certain amount of cargo boiling off each day
which is then used to, usually used in the engines of the ship
to propel the ship.
What's happened as well over time
is that the containment systems have got a lot better.
So if you go back to the steam turbines I was talking about,
you would lose about 0.15 per cent per day of the gas would boil off.
The containment systems today, it's about 0.8 per cent.
So again, basically nearly harving of the boil off rate over the same time.
So what that's done, if you look at what we call the unit freight cost,
how much the cost to deliver each molecule of gas to the customer,
has come down over the last 10, 15 years by about 50 per cent.
Now, if you look at the gas load fleet,
we have, because we've been in the business for quite a long time,
we have ships in each of those different categories.
We have steams primarily at gas load partners.
We have TFDs and we have the two strokes.
The two strokes, which is the more modern ones,
we've probably got one of the largest fleets of those vessels.
All our new bills are modern XDS.
We have 12 of those vessels, all on charter.
When the five which have been built and come out later this year
and beginning into next year come out,
we have around 265 million dollars of EBITDA
from those ships and a total backlog of around 3.8 billion dollars
in total in the company.
Over time, we're looking to continue to build a fleet of modern vessels.
But they're still, in my opinion,
room for the steams and the TFDs
and certainly in intra basing trades and niche trades.
Just a final one for me.
This is one thing I've always been a bit curious about.
It seems that earnings premium for modern vessel
increase the higher the gas prices or energy prices in general.
Is there a chance that this low price gas environment
could make it easier to fix older vessels
on short and medium term employment?
Ja, I think that is, you're correct.
I mean, I think in general, if you look at the market,
we prefer the gas price to be high
because that allows more arbitrage
and more, if you like, rent available for shipping.
But certainly when you get into the lower gas pricing,
the differential between a steam turbine and a modern XDF ship
drops because the amount that you're paying for the gas
or the fuel oil to fuel those ships is so much less.
And as I said, that's especially the case
in the more niche and shorter trades
for intra-basin trades in the area.
For example, the AG into Pakistan, India trades,
where the durations are much shorter.
Those I think of the trades where you can see,
especially when you have low gas prices,
is this that the differential between steams,
TFDs and two strokes drop quite a lot.
So it is helpful in that sense,
but we're looking forward, I think, to a pickup in pricing.
The other thing I would say about LNG pricing
is it is very seasonal,
usually going into the Northern Hemisphere winter,
we see a pickup in pricing,
which we still expect this year.
And I think just generally,
that's helpful in terms of the LNG shipping market.
So I think that wraps it up for today.
So thank you again, Paul,
for taking the time and joining us today.
It will definitely be interesting to continue
to following the LNG shipping sector,
and especially gaslog as well.
Thank you also to you, Mats, for joining us.
And thank you everyone for listening.
Thank you.
Det er som investeringsanbefaling,
eller som investeringsanalyset.
Innollet skal heller ikke anses
for å være investeringsrådgivning,
tilpasset den enkelte kundesbehovet.
Information som gis i podcasten
er kun menet som generell sparverledning,
og er ikke menet å utgjøre juridisk,
finansiell, kommersiell,
skattemessig, eller renskapsmessig rådgivning.
Innollet i sendingen er ikke gjennomgått
eller godkjent av den bemarkedsavdelingen for analyset.
Alle uttalser reflekterer DMBs vurderinger
på tidspunktet for den aktuelle sendingen,
og kan i etterkant endres utnærmere beskjed.
DMB er ikke ansvarlig for hvordan informasjonen i podcasten
benyttes eller tolkes.
Informasjonen som hennviset til historisk avkastning
i finansiell instrumenter,
en finansiell indeks eller prognoser,
gir ingen garantif og fremtidig avkastning.
DMB gjør oppmerksom på at den hver investering
et finansiellest instrument
er forbundet med risiko for økonomisk tap.
Finansielle instrumenter kan både øke og minnske valg,
slik at avkastning kan bli negativ som følg av kursfall.
DMB tar intet ansvar for direkte eller indirekte tap
og kostnader som måtte oppståve bruk av den informasjonen
som gi oss i podcasten.
